commented_code:
+#ifndef HYPERLOGLOG_H
+#define HYPERLOGLOG_H
+
+#include <stdint.h>
+#include <stddef.h>
+#include "object.h"
+
+struct hllhdr {
+    char magic[4];      /* "HYLL" */
+    uint8_t encoding;   /* HLL_DENSE or HLL_SPARSE. */
+    uint8_t notused[3]; /* Reserved for future use, must be zero. */
+    uint8_t card[8];    /* Cached cardinality, little endian. */
+    uint8_t registers[]; /* Data bytes. */
+};

commented_code:
+/*
+ * Key Metadata (keymeta)
+ *
+ * High-level idea
+ * ----------------
+ * keymeta is a framework for attaching & maintaining metadata to keys. 
+ * 
+ * - Up to 8 different metadata classes can be registered globally. First one
+ *   is reserved for EXPIRE.
+ * - Each class has a unique ID and name, like modules datat-types names, yet 
+ *   it has its own namespace.
+ * - Each key metadata class provides a set of callbacks for key lifecycle operations, 
+ *   ensuring consistent handling across copy, rename, logical removal (unlink), 
+ *   actual deallocation (free), persistence (RDB/AOF), and defragmentation. 
+ * - Each key can carry up to 8 independent metadata values. Each value is related 
+ *   to a specific metadata class. 
+ * - The 8-byte slot can either hold inline data or a pointer/handle to a larger, 
+ *   externally managed structure.
+ *
+ * Relation to other components
+ * ----------------------------
+ * - kvobj: 8 bits metabits field in kvobj is used to indicate active metadata.
+ *   bit number corresponds to class ID. 
+ * - Expiration: class ID 0 is reserved for TTL/expire; 
+ * - Registration: redisServer.keyMetaClass[] stores registered classes. Modules
+ *   register via keyMetaClassCreate (see redismodule.h) and may provide callbacks
+ *   for persistence, copy/rename behavior, and lifecycle hooks (unlink/free).
+ * - modules: modules can register metadata classes and provide callbacks.  
+ */
+
+#ifndef __KEYMETA_H
+#define __KEYMETA_H
+
+#include <stdint.h>
+#include <stddef.h>
+#include "sds.h"
+#include "object.h"
+
+/* fwd decls */
+struct redisDb;
+struct redisObject;
+struct RedisModuleIO;
+struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx;
+struct RedisModuleDefragCtx;
+struct RedisModule;
+typedef int KeyMetaClassId; /* Index into redisServer.keyMetaClass[] */
+
+/* kvmeta - Metadata to be attached to kvobj */
+#define KEY_META_ID_EXPIRE        0 /* Must be first */
+/* IDs 1..7 are available for modules */
+#define KEY_META_ID_MODULE_FIRST  1
+#define KEY_META_ID_MODULE_LAST   7
+#define KEY_META_ID_MAX           8
+
+#define KEY_META_MAX_NUM_MODULES  (KEY_META_ID_MODULE_LAST - KEY_META_ID_MODULE_FIRST + 1)
+
+#define KEY_META_MASK_NONE        0
+#define KEY_META_MASK_MODULES     (((1U << KEY_META_MAX_NUM_MODULES) - 1) << KEY_META_ID_MODULE_FIRST)
+#define KEY_META_MASK_EXPIRE      (1U << KEY_META_ID_EXPIRE)
+
+typedef int (*KeyMetaLoadFunc)(RedisModuleIO *rdb, uint64_t *meta, int encver);
+typedef void (*KeyMetaSaveFunc)(RedisModuleIO *rdb, void *value, uint64_t *meta);
+typedef void (*KeyMetaAOFRewriteFunc)(RedisModuleIO *aof, void *value, uint64_t meta);
+typedef void (*KeyMetaFreeFunc)(const char *keyname, uint64_t meta);
+typedef int (*KeyMetaCopyFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, uint64_t *meta);
+typedef int (*KeyMetaRenameFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, uint64_t *meta);
+typedef int (*KeyMetaDefragFunc)(RedisModuleDefragCtx *ctx, RedisModuleString *keyname, uint64_t meta);
+typedef size_t (*KeyMetaMemUsageFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, size_t sample_size, uint64_t meta);
+typedef size_t (*KeyMetaFreeEffortFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, uint64_t meta);
+typedef void (*KeyMetaUnlinkFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, uint64_t *meta);
+typedef int (*KeyMetaMoveFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, uint64_t *meta);
+
+/* For explanation, see struct RedisModuleKeyMetaClassConfig */
+typedef struct KeyMetaClassConf {
+#define KEY_META_FLAG_ALLOW_IGNORE 0   /* Ignore silently on RDB load, if module not avail */
+    uint64_t flags;
+    uint64_t reset_value;

+#define KEY_META_ID_MODULE_LAST   7

+
+typedef int (*KeyMetaLoadFunc)(RedisModuleIO *rdb, uint64_t *meta, int encver);
+typedef void (*KeyMetaSaveFunc)(RedisModuleIO *rdb, void *value, uint64_t *meta);
+typedef void (*KeyMetaAOFRewriteFunc)(RedisModuleIO *aof, void *value, uint64_t meta);
+typedef void (*KeyMetaFreeFunc)(const char *keyname, uint64_t meta);
+typedef int (*KeyMetaCopyFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, uint64_t *meta);

discussion_comments.comment_body: reset_value is essential for preventing double-free bugs when metadata is transferred between keys. When a key is renamed, moved, or copied, the metadata needs to be transferred to the new location AND cleared from the old location. Without reset_value, when the old kvobj is freed, its free callback would try to free the same resource that was already transferred to the new key, causing a crash. The reset_value acts as a sentinel indicating "no metadata here" or "already freed". For example, pointer-based metadata uses reset_value = 0 (NULL), while EXPIRE uses reset_value = -1. You can see this in action in keyMetaOnRename() and keyMetaOnMove() where after transferring metadata to the new key, the old slot is set to reset_value to prevent the free callback from double-freeing.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
this sounds like something that can be resolved differently (like deallocated the meta from the robj at the right time, instead of nullify a pointer to make sure it'll not be re-freed later).
if this hack / complication was just in the implementation, i wouldn't care, but we're burning this into the API, so it looks bad.
i.e. if we can't document what this does for the user (without concerning about redis internal logic), maybe it's an indication that it doesn't belong there.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
I don't see any issue having it in the API. On the contrary, it clarifies the sentinel value of the class. Think about expiration: how nice is it to clarify in the API that -1 is the sentinel... and not 0? The reset_value doesn't expose or involve the module in Redis's internal state management. Module authors don't need to understand that there's a temporary duplication of old and new kv objects and why, or when the old one gets deleted, or why we can't simply reset the metadata bit.

Note, the kv object keeps a bit raised for a given metadata, even after reset, to know how to reach original pointer allocation. We could have extend the kv object to include yet another 8-bit field to track this state separately, but I think we should avoid that complexity. The reset_value approach is simpler: it's a clean contract between Redis and the module. The module defines it once during class registration, and the free callback checks for it - it must to anyway! No internal state tracking needed.


We can improve documentation. Something like:
```
 * * **reset_value**: Sentinel value indicating "no resource to free" for this metadata class.
 *   
 *   This value serves as a marker that the metadata slot exists (for memory management purposes)
 *   but holds no resource that needs cleanup. Your free callback should check if the metadata
 *   equals this value and skip resource cleanup if so, follow this common patterns:
 *   - Pointer-based metadata (e.g., malloc'd strings): use 0 (NULL)
 *   - Handle-based metadata (e.g., file descriptors): use an invalid handle value (e.g., -1)
 *   - Value-based metadata: use a sentinel value that indicates "empty" (e.g., -1 for expiration)
 *   - Primitive metadata (e.g., counters, flags): can be ignored; any value works since there's
 *     no resource to free
 ```

+ * - Registration: redisServer.keyMetaClass[] stores registered classes. Modules
+ *   register via keyMetaClassCreate (see redismodule.h) and may provide callbacks
+ *   for persistence, copy/rename behavior, and lifecycle hooks (unlink/free).
+ * - modules: modules can register metadata classes and provide callbacks.  
+ */
+
+#ifndef __KEYMETA_H
+#define __KEYMETA_H
+
+#include <stdint.h>
+#include <stddef.h>
+#include "sds.h"
+#include "object.h"
+
+/* fwd decls */
+struct redisDb;
+struct redisObject;
+struct RedisModuleIO;
+struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx;
+struct RedisModuleDefragCtx;
+struct RedisModule;
+typedef int KeyMetaClassId; /* Index into redisServer.keyMetaClass[] */
+
+/* kvmeta - Metadata to be attached to kvobj */
+#define KEY_META_ID_EXPIRE        0 /* Must be first */
+/* IDs 1..7 are available for modules */
+#define KEY_META_ID_MODULE_FIRST  1
+#define KEY_META_ID_MODULE_LAST   7
+#define KEY_META_ID_MAX           8
+
+#define KEY_META_MAX_NUM_MODULES  (KEY_META_ID_MODULE_LAST - KEY_META_ID_MODULE_FIRST + 1)
+
+#define KEY_META_MASK_NONE        0
+#define KEY_META_MASK_MODULES     (((1U << KEY_META_MAX_NUM_MODULES) - 1) << KEY_META_ID_MODULE_FIRST)
+#define KEY_META_MASK_EXPIRE      (1U << KEY_META_ID_EXPIRE)
+
+typedef int (*KeyMetaLoadFunc)(RedisModuleIO *rdb, uint64_t *meta, int encver);
+typedef void (*KeyMetaSaveFunc)(RedisModuleIO *rdb, void *value, uint64_t *meta);
+typedef void (*KeyMetaAOFRewriteFunc)(RedisModuleIO *aof, void *value, uint64_t meta);
+typedef void (*KeyMetaFreeFunc)(const char *keyname, uint64_t meta);
+typedef int (*KeyMetaCopyFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, uint64_t *meta);
+typedef int (*KeyMetaRenameFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, uint64_t *meta);
+typedef int (*KeyMetaDefragFunc)(RedisModuleDefragCtx *ctx, RedisModuleString *keyname, uint64_t meta);
+typedef size_t (*KeyMetaMemUsageFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, size_t sample_size, uint64_t meta);
+typedef size_t (*KeyMetaFreeEffortFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, uint64_t meta);
+typedef void (*KeyMetaUnlinkFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, uint64_t *meta);
+typedef int (*KeyMetaMoveFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, uint64_t *meta);
+
+/* For explanation, see struct RedisModuleKeyMetaClassConfig */
+typedef struct KeyMetaClassConf {
+#define KEY_META_FLAG_ALLOW_IGNORE 0   /* Ignore silently on RDB load, if module not avail */
+    uint64_t flags;
+    uint64_t reset_value;
discussion_comments.comment_body:
you're saying we need to choose between wasting another bit per type, or wasting just one value (out of 2^64 possible combinations), i'm arguing that with some extra re-allocation we don't have to "allocate" either of these.
i think this is wrong to expose this concern to the API (considering that AFAIK the overhead of reallocating the object to exclude that meta entirely, in this rare case is low, and i suppose we can also find other options).
also, you can consider that some day you'll have a module that needs all 64 bits and all 2^64 combinations, and could conflict with that.

it's hard to predict if this sentinel concept is gonna come in handy some day or cause harm. i think that it is logically wrong, but i won't stand in your way if you disagree.

+ * - Each class has a unique ID and name, like modules datat-types names, yet

+ * - Each key can carry up to 8 independent metadata values. Each value is related 
+ *   to a specific metadata class. 
+ * - The 8-byte slot can either hold inline data or a pointer/handle to a larger, 
+ *   externally managed structure.
+ *
+ * Relation to other components
+ * ----------------------------
+ * - kvobj: 8 bits metabits field in kvobj is used to indicate active metadata.
+ *   bit number corresponds to class ID. 
+ * - Expiration: class ID 0 is reserved for TTL/expire; 
+ * - Registration: redisServer.keyMetaClass[] stores registered classes. Modules
+ *   register via keyMetaClassCreate (see redismodule.h) and may provide callbacks
+ *   for persistence, copy/rename behavior, and lifecycle hooks (unlink/free).
+ * - modules: modules can register metadata classes and provide callbacks.  
+ */

+

+#include <stddef.h>

discussion_comments.comment_body:
> or wasting just one value

You don't need to waste any value:
- If it is primitive, then this `reset_value` goanna be ignored anyway on free. And all 64bits are available for manipulation. (I would claim that primitve also should have natural `reset_value` ...)
- Pointer/handle metadata: These already have a natural invalid sentinel (e.g., NULL or -1). The API just makes that explicit in one place (class registration), rather than relying on implicit assumptions in each free callback.

Beyond metadata API, kvobj can be temporarily duplicated for various reasons, not only meta-related, and freed sync/async. We need a reliable way for the old instance’s free callback to know there’s no resource here anymore. `reset_value` is the minimal, explicit contract for that, without adding another per-object state bit or doing extra reallocations just to drop a meta slot (...no point to realloc as we about to delete temporary kvobj).

(Wrote it here for doc. Will try to reach you to talk 1x1 further next week. Thx.)

discussion_comments.comment_body:
If the module keeps metadata on **RENAME** and **MOVE** , then the free callback will encounter `reset_value` only when freeing the `source` key after a successful transfer operation. This prevents double-free since the pointer was transferred to the destination key. i.e., later on the free callback will be invoked on `source` with trivial `reset_value`.

Another option is to conditionally call all callbacks only if metadata doesn't equal `reset_value`. This will solve the double-free problem and simplify the logic for module authors. That is, before invoking any callback (free, unlink, copy, rename, move, rdb_save, …), Redis checks if meta == `reset_value` and skips the callback if true.

**Pros:**
* Eliminates double-free scenarios automatically without module authors needing to remember check `reset_value`.
* Reduced number of callbacks involved. Redis guarantees they only receive valid metadata!
* reset_value universally means "no resource attached" across all operations
* Prevents persisting sentinel values to RDB/AOF !
* Avoids unnecessary callback invocations when there's no resource to process

**Cons:**
* Forces `reset_value` for primitives: Even if metadata represents a primitive value (counter, flags, timestamp) where all values are semantically valid, the module must designate a reset_value. However, this is a minor inconvenience since primitive metadata typically doesn't need a free callback anyway and most primitive types have a natural sentinel (e.g., 0, -1, UINT64_MAX)

IMO, the benefits significantly outweigh the minor constraint on primitive metadata. The approach makes the common case safe by default while still supporting primitive values with minimal overhead.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
i'm ok with all 3 options.
3rd being not to expose this interface, and let the module handle it (while letting it set the source on Move to NULL, and always duplicating on Copy, in a way the module is the only one that knows its own sentinel value).

i do have some fear that if you'll avoid calling all callbacks when the meta == reset_value, we might face some complication that the module will be unable to resolve (missing a callback to tell them it's empty). but maybe logically, that's the right way and they'll have to work around that if they need to. so i'm ok with the option you suggested (if we have a reset_value, that's an acceptable way to handle it)

+typedef int (*KeyMetaDefragFunc)(RedisModuleDefragCtx *ctx, RedisModuleString *keyname, uint64_t meta);
+typedef size_t (*KeyMetaMemUsageFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, size_t sample_size, uint64_t meta);
+typedef size_t (*KeyMetaFreeEffortFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, uint64_t meta);
+typedef void (*KeyMetaUnlinkFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, uint64_t *meta);
+typedef int (*KeyMetaMoveFunc)(struct RedisModuleKeyOptCtx *ctx, uint64_t *meta);
+
+/* For explanation, see struct RedisModuleKeyMetaClassConfig */
+typedef struct KeyMetaClassConf {
+#define KEY_META_FLAG_ALLOW_IGNORE 0   /* Ignore silently on RDB load, if module not avail */
+    uint64_t flags;
+    uint64_t reset_value;
discussion_comments.comment_body: So I will go with "conditionally call all callbacks only if metadata doesn't equal `reset_value`". Thanks.
The config ownership will remain on the module side right? so the scope of the *conf should be the same as the ClassID?
Redis makes a copy of the config struct. that is, the config ownership remains on the module side and scope of "config" is relevant only for the call.

I am not sure this API is complete. What if for key we use an allocated struct with something that we need to protect with a r/w lock mutex. Then we would need to have control of the copy process? not only saying true or false if we want to copy?

Or is the idea that if is a dynamic object, that u keep ownership of these and in the uint u only pass the address to these objects? and then in the copy callback u decide if the object is shared between copies or not?

The module has full control over the metadata maintenance via callbacks from Redis. Whether to have a lock or not is entirely an implementation decision by the module. Most callbacks run on the main thread (GIL-protected), requiring no module locks. Only the free callback may run in a background thread, so modules must use thread-safe operations there if accessing shared resources.

@oranagra please see if you have anything to comment on this matter.

Sure, my question was how would u copy the value if we return simply 1 in this case, I guess this would only be valid for simple uint64_t cases?

In case we have more complex cases that are handled on module side, u expect us to treat the `*meta` as a pointer, we should allocate a new object, copy its values, and update meta to point to the copy? Is this the expected usage?

Is there an example of this in some tests?

@JoanFM i really hope modules will just store meta data here, and there will be no cases of cross key relationship, pointers to global tables, and read write locks (or any locks), so i'd rather not think about it.

are you saying you have one for search (don't you just store a doc-id)? or are you asking a hypothetical question?
it is hypothetical. We aim to store an array or hashmap of DocIDs
maybe we should use `void *userdata` instead of `struct asmTrimCtx *trim_ctx`

`void *userdata` makes sense for callback signatures that need to be generic with zero knowledge, but flushCommandCommon is a concrete function that always knows it's dealing with an asmTrimCtx and need to access this struct. It also gives us compile time type safety and documented API
i just feel the `struct asmTrimCtx` is not closely related to `flushCommandCommon`. no strong opinion, it is up to you

I agree it's not the most natural fit, but void *userdata just to hide the type feels worse. It's only SFLUSH that needs it, and if later we need to pass more data, we can pack it into a more general-purpose struct at that point. Now I prefer to avoid prematurely generalize.
